## Sub-requirements

### 8.1.1 — DPO-catalysed workforce data-protection awareness with Art. 39(1)(a)+(b) documentation

```yaml
- req_id: 8.1.1
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "Personnel authorised to process personal data receive DPO-catalysed awareness-raising and training on GDPR obligations applicable to their role"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-8.1.GDPR
    per_reg_so: SO-GDPR-025
    source_sr: SR-GDPR-042
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-08_Human-Factors/8.1.md
    objective_ref_section: "### 8.1.1"

  description: |
    The product shall operate a DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness programme covering personnel authorised to process personal data, with the DPO informing and advising the controller or processor and the employees who carry out processing of their obligations (Art. 39(1)(a) GDPR) and monitoring compliance including assignment of responsibilities, awareness-raising and training of staff involved in processing operations (Art. 39(1)(b) GDPR), where the controller is the data-protection awareness anchor on the controller-side processing-staff audience.

  fit_criterion: |
    A DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness programme is operated covering all personnel authorised to process personal data via PR.AT-01 broad-coverage leg (phishing, social-engineering, password-discipline, removable-media, incident-reporting reflexes as they apply to data-protection obligations) on a recurring cadence with content that updates to reflect emerging threats and the changing data flows of the organisation; the DPO inform-and-advise obligation under Art. 39(1)(a) is documented with documented advisory records (DPO briefs + induction material + privacy-notice change advisory); the DPO awareness-raising and training obligation under Art. 39(1)(b) is documented with annual induction-completion records + periodic reinforcement records (DPO-discretionary cadence under Recital 56 — no quantitative OJ cadence; `awareness-raising` implies ongoing reinforcement rather than one-off induction per SR-GDPR-042 ambiguity reading); personnel with data-subject-facing, access-management, breach-response or DPO-deputy responsibilities receive role-specific reinforcement of their GDPR and cybersecurity obligations under PR.AT-02 (8.2 layer — cross-ref 8.2.1); the programme is documented under Art. 5(2) accountability so the controller can demonstrate on the day of any supervisory inspection that the Art. 39(1)(a)+(b) DPO-catalysed awareness function was operationally exercised and recorded; for an integrated controller-NSIE workforce the GDPR data-protection module combines with the NIS 2 entity-wide basic-hygiene module into a single curriculum where the data-protection module feeds the GDPR obligation and the hygiene module feeds the NIS 2 obligation (per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔NIS2 verified relationship — separate evidence trails on the controller side vs the entity side); for a DORA financial entity that is a controller processing personal data over its own staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness is a sub-component of the carrier (per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔DORA verified relationship — DORA programme as carrier + GDPR content as module); for a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a controller, the GDPR workforce awareness obligation is independent of the CRA Annex II §8(a)–(f) user-facing instructions (substantively scope-disjoint audiences — workforce vs external end-user — per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔CRA verified relationship — no consolidation rule applies); verified by DPO-catalysed awareness-records inspection + DPO inform-and-advise records review + Art. 5(2) accountability evidence pack + training-cadence documentation + DPA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [8.1.2 (when controller = NSIE — GDPR data-protection module combines with NIS 2 hygiene module into single curriculum), 8.1.4 (when DORA-controller — DORA Art. 13(6) is the carrier programme; GDPR content is a sub-component)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AT-01, PR.AT-02]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [GDPR]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # GDPR↔NIS2 = Y (controller-NSIE; data-protection module + hygiene module combined); GDPR↔CRA = N (typically) substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs end-user audiences); GDPR↔DORA = Y (financial-entity-controller; DORA programme as carrier + GDPR content as module); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The GDPR general-awareness obligation is the **DPO-driven catalyst for the controller-side awareness programme**, distinct from the controller's substantive training obligation — the DPO operates as the internal advisor and awareness-raising/training agent for staff involved in processing (Art. 39(1)(a) + Art. 39(1)(b)). The design-anchor is DPO-discretionary cadence (no quantitative cadence in Art. 39(1)(b); the OJ is silent on training-frequency or completion-thresholds); `awareness-raising` implies ongoing reinforcement rather than one-off induction (per SR-GDPR-042 ambiguity reading). The DPO's awareness-raising obligation is structurally a general-population training duty covering all personnel involved in processing, on a recurring cadence and with content that updates to reflect emerging threats and the changing data flows of the organisation (PR.AT-01 anchor). The role-specific layer (PR.AT-02) covers personnel with data-subject-facing, access-management, breach-response, or DPO-deputy responsibilities — distinct from the general awareness programme and discharged at the 8.2 layer (8.2.1). The DPO's awareness function is documented under Art. 5(2) accountability on the controller side — the controller must demonstrate that the Art. 39(1)(a)+(b) DPO-catalysed awareness function was operationally exercised and recorded on the day of any supervisory inspection. **8.2 layer (RE...-8.2-GDPR)** adds the role-specific depth on the same workforce. For an integrated controller-NSIE workforce, the GDPR data-protection module combines with the NIS 2 entity-wide hygiene baseline into a single curriculum (per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔NIS2 verified relationship). For a DORA financial entity that is a controller processing personal data over its own staff, the DORA Art. 13(6) programme is the carrier and the GDPR Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness is the module (per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔DORA verified relationship). For a CRA-regulated manufacturer that is also a controller, the GDPR workforce awareness obligation is independent of the CRA Annex II §8(a)–(f) user-facing instructions (substantively scope-disjoint audiences — workforce vs end-user — no consolidation rule applies). The recipient is the supervisory authority (DPA) — Art. 5(2) accountability on the Art. 39(1)(b) DPO-catalysed awareness/training records + Art. 30(4) staff awareness record + Art. 47(2)(n) BCR training (cross to 9.2 via Art. 35 DPIA necessary-assessment chain). (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### 8.1.1`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 8.1.2 — Entity-wide basic-hygiene baseline with `appropriate and proportionate` calibration

```yaml
- req_id: 8.1.2
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "Entity-wide basic cyber-hygiene practices with workforce hygiene substrate and proportionate calibration"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-8.1.NIS2
    per_reg_so: SO-NIS2-018
    source_sr: SR-NIS2-029
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-08_Human-Factors/8.1.md
    objective_ref_section: "### 8.1.2"

  description: |
    The product shall establish basic cyber-hygiene practices and cybersecurity training for all users of network and information systems with access to entity systems and services (Art. 21(2)(g) first clause NIS 2), with the depth calibrated by the Art. 21(1) `appropriate and proportionate` chapeau and the state-of-the-art floor, on the entity-wide workforce-and-systems substrate, and with Art. 20(1) management-body approval+oversight+liability.

  fit_criterion: |
    An entity-wide basic-hygiene programme is operated covering all users of network and information systems with access to entity systems and services via the human-side hygiene substrate PR.AT-01 (phishing awareness, password discipline, social-engineering recognition, removable-media hygiene, disciplined use of administrative privileges) plus the system-side hygiene substrate PR.PS-01 (baseline configurations, secure configuration standards, change management and patch cadence for the NIS inventory); the two together operationalise the ENISA / SANS Top 20 / Australian Cyber Security Centre Essential Eight convergence on workforce-and-assets hygiene; the chosen reading of `basic cyber hygiene` is the broader ENISA baseline reading, with the chosen reading being entity-discretionary interpretation under Recital 56 proportionality (no quantitative cadence in Art. 21(2)(g) and no OJ enumeration — three operative readings admitted per SR-NIS2-029 ambiguity_notes); the depth is calibrated by the Art. 21(1) `appropriate and proportionate` chapeau + state-of-the-art floor + Recital 56 proportionality factors (nature/size/risk profile/cost of implementation); for an integrated controller-NSIE workforce the NIS 2 hygiene module combines with the GDPR DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness module into a single curriculum (per CRDA-deep §8.1 GDPR↔NIS2 verified relationship — combined curriculum with separate evidence trails); for DORA financial entities, the DORA Art. 13(6) `compulsory modules` exceeds NIS 2 `basic cyber hygiene` and the DORA programme discharges the NIS 2 workforce-awareness obligation (per CRDA-deep §8.1 NIS2↔DORA EQUAL classification — DORA-dominant for financial entities, NIS 2-dominant for non-financial NSIEs); for a CRA-regulated manufacturer, the NIS 2 hygiene baseline on the workforce is independent of the CRA Annex II §8(a)–(f) user-facing instructions (substantively scope-disjoint audiences — workforce vs end-user — no consolidation rule); the Art. 20(1) management-body approval is documented as the governance hook; verified by entity-wide hygiene-coverage audit + PR.AT-01 + PR.PS-01 records review + Art. 21(1) proportionality calibration documentation + Art. 20(1) management-body approval records + management-body + competent-authority inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [8.1.1 (when controller = NSIE — NIS 2 hygiene module combines with GDPR data-protection module into single curriculum), 8.1.4 (when DORA-NSIE — DORA Art. 13(6) discharges NIS 2 workforce-awareness obligation for financial entities)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AT-01, PR.PS-01]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [NIS2]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # NIS2↔GDPR = Y (controller-NSIE; combined curriculum); NIS2↔CRA = N (typically) substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs end-user); NIS2↔DORA = Y (DORA financial entity = NSIE; DORA programme discharges NIS 2; for pure-financial NIS 2 §9 fall-back does not apply — DORA lex specialis); HL engages on overlap

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The NIS 2 general-awareness obligation is the **entity-wide hygiene baseline** covering all users of network and information systems with access to entity systems and services (per SO-NIS2-018 scope). The hygiene practices are not enumerated in the OJ — `basic cyber hygiene` admits three operative readings (minimal-password-patch-antivirus, broader ENISA baseline, sector-specific), with the chosen reading being entity-discretionary interpretation under Recital 56 proportionality (per SR-NIS2-029 ambiguity_notes). PR.AT-01 anchors the human-side hygiene substrate (phishing awareness, password discipline, social-engineering recognition, removable-media hygiene, disciplined use of administrative privileges). PR.PS-01 anchors the system-side hygiene substrate (baseline configurations, secure configuration standards, change management and patch cadence for the NIS inventory). The two together define the workforce-and-assets hygiene baseline that ENISA, the SANS Top 20, and the Australian Cyber Security Centre Essential Eight converge on (per SR-NIS2-029 security_rationale). The two controls also propagate into the vulnerability-hygiene loop: hygiene lapses are themselves a recurring source of incidents that ID.RA-01 records and PR.PS-02 remediates. **8.2 layer (8.2.2)** adds the Member-State-encouraged employee training on risk-identification and assessment per Art. 20(2) sentence 2. For an integrated controller-NSIE workforce, the NIS 2 hygiene module combines with the GDPR DPO-catalysed data-protection awareness module into a single curriculum. For DORA financial entities, the DORA Art. 13(6) `compulsory modules` is more prescriptive than NIS 2's `basic cyber hygiene` (entity-discretionary under Recital 56) — DORA-dominant for financial entities; NIS 2-dominant for non-financial NSIEs (per CRDA-deep §8.1 NIS2↔DORA EQUAL classification). For a CRA-regulated manufacturer, the NIS 2 hygiene baseline on the workforce is independent of the CRA Annex II §8(a)–(f) user-facing instructions (substantively scope-disjoint audiences — workforce vs end-user — no consolidation rule applies). The recipient is the management body (Art. 20(1) approve+oversee+liability) + competent authority (Art. 21(1) + Art. 21(2)(g) basic-hygiene records). (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### 8.1.2`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 8.1.3 — Manufacturer-shipped detailed user-facing secure-use instructions with 10-year support-period accessibility

```yaml
- req_id: 8.1.3
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "Product with digital elements accompanied by detailed user-facing secure-use instructions covering the lifetime of the product"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-8.1.CRA
    per_reg_so: SO-CRA-048 (partial)
    source_sr: SR-CRA-071
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-08_Human-Factors/8.1.md
    objective_ref_section: "### 8.1.3"

  description: |
    The product with digital elements shall be accompanied by detailed instructions — or an internet address referring to such detailed instructions — covering the necessary measures during initial commissioning and throughout the lifetime of the product to ensure its secure use (Annex II §8(a)–(e) CRA), and — where the product is intended for integration into other products — the information necessary for the integrator to comply with the essential cybersecurity requirements set out in Part I of Annex I and the documentation requirements set out in Annex VII (Annex II §8(f) CRA), with a duty to provide the instructions in paper or electronic form accessible for the support period under Art. 13(18).

  fit_criterion: |
    The product with digital elements is accompanied by detailed secure-use instructions under Annex II §8(a) initial-commissioning + lifetime measures, §8(b) how changes to the product affect data security, §8(c) how security-relevant updates are installed, §8(d) secure decommissioning including user-data removal, §8(e) how the default automatic-update setting can be turned off, and §8(f) — where intended for integration — the information necessary for the integrator to comply with Annex I + Annex VII; the instructions are provided in paper or electronic form and remain accessible for the support period ≥ 5 years under Art. 13(8) sentence 3 + 10-year Annex II information accessibility under Art. 13(18); the CRA participation at 8.1 is partial — Annex II §8(a)–(f) addresses the end-user audience with the product (not workforce) per AEGIS taxonomy §4.1; the workforce-side partial dimension is the Annex II §8(f) integrator information for B2B integrators who incorporate the product into a downstream system (information counterpart to a contractually-anchored SCRM commitment per SR-CRA-108 security_rationale — Annex II §8(f) integrates at the 8.2 layer); the `detailed` content threshold is harmonised-standards-pending (no OJ definition of `detailed`); the chosen reading of `Throughout the lifetime of the product` is the entire supported lifetime + 10-year update tail (per SR-CRA-071 ambiguity reading); verified by Annex II §8(a)–(f) instruction-set review + Art. 13(18) support-period accessibility audit + Annex II §8(f) integrator-information review (B2B integrators) + support-period ≥ 5 years + Annex VII §2(b) + §3 + §6 + §8 technical-documentation inspection + market surveillance authority (MSA) / ENISA inspection-ready evidence pack on request.

  priority: MUST
  verification_method: TEST
  dependencies: [8.2.3 (Annex II §8(f) integrator information integrates at the 8.2 role-specific layer — integrator-B2B audience)]

  nist_csf: [PR.AT-01]

  applicable_if:
    regs: [CRA]
    scope_overlap: Conditional   # CRA↔GDPR = N (typically) substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs end-user audiences); CRA↔NIS2 = N (typically) substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs end-user); CRA↔DORA = N (typically) substantively scope-disjoint (end-user vs internal staff); HL engages only on Y-pairs (none of the CRA-involving pairs engage the workforce-side HL)

  satisfaction:
    controls: []
    nodes: []

  considerations: |
    The CRA general-awareness obligation at 8.1 is the **product-side Annex II §8 detailed-instructions package** — user-facing (Annex II §8(a)–(e)) + integrator-facing B2B (Annex II §8(f)). The Annex II §8(a)–(e) user-side addresses the end-user with the product — not the manufacturer's or operator's workforce. The Annex II §8(f) integrator information is the **workforce-side partial dimension** — integrators who incorporate the product into a downstream system are informed of the cybersecurity measures required to maintain Annex I compliance (B2B integrators are the closest workforce-side dimension at 8.1). The CRA participation at 8.1 is partial: Annex II §8 is user-facing not workforce; AEGIS taxonomy §4.1 marks NIS 2 / DORA / GDPR as the authorities for full staff awareness. Art. 13(18) extends this to a duty to provide the instructions in paper or electronic form, accessible for the support period (10-year Annex II information accessibility; cross to 9.4). The 3 CRA-involving pairs (GDPR↔CRA, NIS2↔CRA, CRA↔DORA) are substantively scope-disjoint (workforce vs end-user audiences) — the parallel tracks coexist on different audiences with no consolidation rule; the high-level SO does NOT collapse the audience boundaries. **8.2 layer (8.2.3)** adds the role-specific depth for the integrator-B2B audience via Annex II §8(f) (integrator-facing information on threat model + configuration constraints + vulnerability-handling interface). The recipient is the end-user (B2C/user-facing) + market surveillance authority (MSA) — Annex II §8(a)–(f) instructions on the product; Annex II §8(f) integrator information discharge the workforce-side partial dimension for B2B integrators. (Copied from the HSO Considerations block of `### 8.1.3`; preserved here for self-contained traceability.)
```

### 8.1.4 — Financial-entity compulsory-modules-led ICT security awareness and resilience training

```yaml
- req_id: 8.1.4
  volere_section: 15
  volere_subtype: immunity

  title: "ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in financial-entity staff training schemes"

  derives_from:
    hso_sub_so: SO-8.1.DORA
    per_reg_so: SO-DORA-015
    source_sr: SR-DORA-025
    hso_file: ../../HierarchicalSecurityObjectives/D-08_Human-Factors/8.1.md
    objective_ref_section: "### 8.1.4"

  description: |
    The product shall develop ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in the financial-entity's staff training schemes (Art. 13(6) DORA), with a level of complexity commensurate to the remit of staff functions, on the financial-entity's workforce audience, with a digital-operational-resilience-testing-aligned syllabus and Art. 5(2) management-body oversight.

    The DORA general-awareness obligation is the **financial-entity-side compulsory-modules-led workforce training programme** — Art. 13(6) `ICT security awareness programmes and digital operational resilience training as compulsory modules in their staff training schemes, with a level of complexity commensurate to the remit of their functions`. PR.AT-01 anchors the baseline awareness layer that closes the human-factor gap (phishing recognition, social-engineering resistance, password hygiene, incident-reporting reflexes) and converts the OJ `ICT security awareness programmes` from an opt-in continuing-professional-development activity into a compulsory training-module floor across the workforce. PR.AT-02 layers the role-specific depth dimension that the OJ `commensurate to the remit of their functions` qualifier demands (8.2 layer — 8.2.4), ensuring staff in security-critical roles receive calibrated depth rather than a uniform baseline. The DORA programme co-exists with GDPR Art. 39 staff-awareness duties for personal-data processing and with NIS 2 Art. 21(2)(g) basic security training baselines — EBA Guidelines on ICT and security risk management set out the supervisory expectation on calibration. For DORA financial entities deploying/manufacturing CRA products, the DORA Art. 13(6) internal staff training is independent of the CRA Annex II §8(f) integrator-facing information (substantively scope-disjoint audiences). ## Decisions (CORR-029)
```

### Orphan SR justifications

**CRA**: 4 SRs (SR-CRA-071, SR-CRA-107, SR-CRA-110, SR-CRA-111) target D-08.1 but no CRA SO exists. Per source MD line 39, CRA is 'partial coverage' (Annex II §8 user-instructions only — not workforce awareness). The CRA-side user-instructions obligation is preserved as a partial-coverage cross-ref to the D-08.1 awareness layer; the primary CRA SO is SR-CRA-048 (Annex II §8 user-instructions) which is anchored in D-08.1 for the user-audience dimension only. No standalone CRA SO for D-08.1 is needed because the workforce-awareness obligation is not in the OJ text; Annex II §8 instructions are user-facing, not workforce-facing. CORR-030 will consider whether to create an explicit partial-coverage CRA SO here or to re-anchor these SRs to a different subdomain.
